claim: The high hapax ratio (70.1%) is substantially artifactual: word-final glyphs drawn from the set {y, n, l, r, o} function as grammatical suffixes, and stripping them would reduce the effective vocabulary by at least 30%, collapsing hapax rate to below 50%.
evidence_for: Word-final glyph constraints show {y,n,l,r,o} account for the vast majority of word endings, consistent with a small set of suffix tokens
Top frequent words like 'daiin', 'aiin', 'chedy', 'shedy' share common endings suggesting paradigmatic inflection
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is below 1.0, consistent with a vocabulary inflated by suffix variants of stem words
evidence_against: If suffixes were purely grammatical, we would expect cleaner paradigm clusters around common stems
Hapax rate of 70.1% is extremely high even for inflected languages with suffixes stripped
Entropy of 3.8627 bits is close to natural language levels, not obviously inflated by suffix tokens
test: Compute vocabulary size and hapax ratio after stripping all word-final characters belonging to {y, n, l, r, o}; compare suffix-stripped Zipf exponent and type-token ratio to baseline
test_metric: Hapax ratio and vocabulary size of suffix-stripped corpus vs. original
test_threshold: Confirmation if hapax ratio drops below 50% after stripping; denial if hapax ratio remains above 60%

tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If suffixes account for most lexical diversity, confirming this would collapse a major anomaly and strongly constrain the cipher model toward an inflected-language substitution scheme
